originalEntry.summary: King of the gods, god of sky and thunder.

enhancedEntry.summary: Supreme king of the Olympian gods, ruler of the cosmos and embodiment of divine authority, justice, and cosmic order. As the wielder of thunderbolts and patron of hospitality, Zeus maintains the fundamental balance between celestial power and earthly governance, while embodying the complex duality of sovereign justice and personal capriciousness that defines Greek theological understanding of divine rulership.
enhancedEntry.culturalContext: Central figure of Greek religion and state identity, Zeus represents the transition from tribal chiefdom to city-state governance, with his worship practices evolving from local cults to Pan-Hellenic religious synthesis that influenced Roman imperial cult and later Christian theological concepts of divine kingship.
enhancedEntry.narrativeImportance: Zeus appears in foundational myths including the Titanomachy (cosmic succession), the Prometheus cycle (divine justice vs human progress), and numerous transformation myths where his actions establish precedents for divine law, cosmic order, and the proper relationship between gods and mortals. His character serves as the archetype through which Greek culture explored themes of power, justice, authority, and the limits of divine intervention.

relationships.nature: Son who overthrows father, establishing new cosmic order
Divine marriage representing sacred union and cosmic partnership
Divine authority vs human progress, punishment for benefaction
relationships.myth: Prometheus fire theft and eternal punishment

relationships.nature: Mother consumed, prophecy of overthrow by son
relationships.myth: Metis prophecy and Zeus's birth strategy
